figures.caption: Table 11: Average RMSE for the bottom 20% of agents using the greedy and decentralized (Nash) strategies for RFN (top) and ESN (bottom) models. Numbers in parentheses denote the total agent pool size, where results are averaged over the 20 worst-performing agents for a pool of 100, and over the 100 worst-performing agents for a pool of 500.
Table 9: Best hyperparameters for each dataset with the greedy strategies for ESNs models.

Table 10: Average RMSE for aggregated RFN (top) and ESN (bottom) model predictions using the greedy strategy with 100 agents. For θ ∈ {0.9, 0.7}, the best HP is used of the two. When θ = 1.0, this implicitly means θ̄ = 0.
Table 7: Best hyperparameters for each dataset with the greedy strategy for RFN models.
Table 8: Best hyperparameters for each dataset with the decentralized strategies for ESNs models.
Figure 1: Aggregated predictions compared with targets for RFN models with and without the decentralized strategy for the Logistic Map (left) and BoC Validation (right) timeseries.
Table 1: Average RMSE for aggregated RFN model predictions using the greedy and decentralized (Nash) strategies. Numbers in parentheses indicate the number of agents in the mixture.
Table 6: Best hyperparameters for each dataset with the decentralized strategy for RFN models.
Table 4: Average RMSE for uniformly averaged ESN predictions using the greedy and decentralized (Nash) strategies. Numbers in parentheses indicate the number of agents in the mixture.
Table 3: Average RMSE for the worst agent using the greedy and decentralized (Nash) strategies for RFN (top) and ESN (bottom) models. Numbers in parentheses indicate the size of the agent pool.
Table 2: Average RMSE for aggregated ESN model predictions using the greedy and decentralized (Nash) strategies. Numbers in parentheses indicate the number of agents in the mixture.
